cards.back: affect = usually the verb (to influence); effect = usually the noun (the result).
fewer for countable (fewer bugs); less for uncountable (less code).
i.e. = "that is" (clarify); e.g. = "for example".
who = subject; whom = object. If you could answer "him", use whom.
its = belonging to it; it's = it is / it has.
your = belonging to you; you're = you are.
then = time/sequence; than = comparison.
complement completes; compliment is praise.
You lay an object down; you lie down yourself (no object).
principal = main / head person; principle = a rule.
there = place; their = belonging to them; they're = they are.
accept = to receive; except = apart from / excluding.
lose = to not win / misplace; loose = not tight.
whose = belonging to whom; who's = who is / who has.
to = direction/infinitive; too = also / excessively; two = 2.
led = past tense of "lead"; "lead" (the metal) just sounds the same.
everyday = ordinary (adjective); every day = each day.
farther = physical distance; further = figurative/extent.
The speaker implies (hints); the listener infers (concludes).
assure a person; ensure a result; insure against financial loss.
me — after a preposition use the object: "between you and me".
No — it's "could have" / "could've". "of" just sounds like "'ve".
To join two related full sentences: "It rained; we stayed in."
The comma before "and" in a list: "a, b, and c" — prevents ambiguity.
